Number three, really every time we put extra attention on really bumping up the proof, we would bump up our response.

This is the one that gives you the most leverage because it's the one that most advertises overlook. ## [12:04 - 15:06]

The greatest secrets of building belief. Well, first good example is demonstration. Claude Hopkins once said that no argument in the world can ever compare with one dramatic demonstration. Many of you people have written in an Otis elevator, I'm sure. You know, as we get on the elevator and other people crowd in, everybody stays at their shoe tops, you know, not wanting to make eye contact with anybody. You might look around and happen to notice the Otis elevator brass sign on many elevators. Otis did not invent the elevator. What Otis invented was a way for elevators to stop themselves when the cables broke because in the 1800s, people who got on elevators and a cable broke, they had a very short lifespan. It was about four or five flights and that was it. And people really were afraid of even going on elevators because if the rope cables broke, they would plunge to their death. The United States, in the country, including New York, any buildings up until the 1850s were only about three or four stories high. This is the demonstration that changed the world. I'll show you how. Otis invented an automatic braking system where even if the cables suddenly got severed, it would only drop an inch or two and then a mechanism would kick in and stop the elevator from falling any further. So he announced this to the world and expected to get rich immediately and all the Otis' started coming in. Something happened. People said, yes, sure, you got a great system with your elevator. I'm taking the stairs. Thank you very much. I've heard a lot of promises before and I don't want to end up in the basement under a lot of debris. So he didn't know what to do. In 1855, two years later, he said, I've got to do something. He set up a demonstration and as you'll see, it was almost like a world's fair of the day. It was called the Crystal Palace Exposition in New York in 1855 with thousands of people milling down the midway. He had this big contraption set up. He was going to rise in an open elevator. That's Otis with a top hat. He had an assistant. This is such a famous demonstration that's been immortalized in artwork and so on. He had an assistant on top with an axe. So they gathered a big crowd. They said, look what I've invented. Nobody is believing what I've done so let me just show you. And he would say, are we ready? And they'd lift them way up and they say, cut the cable and the assistant would whack one cable and crowd with gasp. And he said, wait, I'm still held up by one cable. The assistant would go over to the other side and with an axe, whack the other cable and people would really gasp because he was about to plunge to his death from their assumption of what happened to an elevator. He would fall an inch or two and stop. He says, do you see now what I've invented? He says, you never have to worry about getting on an elevator again. And so that one demonstration had made all the headlines and the newspapers and from that day forward, people were no longer worried about getting on an elevator.

It takes time to figure out proof rather than just add high P adjectives. So this is where your prospects really know whether you're BSing them or not. And so your prospects are looking for this because they know that proof is the rarest element of persuasion and what is rare is most prized. So your prospects are looking for the most prized part of your presentation which is the proof. And you're the same way, and if you're going to hire somebody that can give you a lot of promises, you want to know proof. When I give you my proof, then you may be interested in coming, that I have a lot of control packages and so on. So this is the most rare and therefore most prized element of persuasion that all your prospects are desperately looking for in your ads. It's the best area for you to be looking in which is why I made a proof element marketing. I mean, you still have to offer a strong promise. He was a partner with somebody else in a pizza joint where he was going to college. And his friend kind of left the business or moved away. And so he bought him out. And he came up with this interesting promise. Fresh hot pizza delivered in 30 minutes or less guaranteed.

## [21:12 - 24:12]

Very strong proof element. Now, notice what he's not talking about. He's not talking about mama's sauce recipe from the old country. He's not talking about the extra crispy crust. He's not talking about what most people are talking about. So he's talking about something really unique and a proof element that's really unique. And you know, within a number of years, just a college student now, he became one of the richest men in the world. Because within a matter of years, based on that guarantee, his thought was, pizza really don't care about mama's sauce in the old country when they're hungry for a pizza. They're really not fast. So let me focus on that promise. In 30 minutes or less guaranteed, you don't pay for the pizza. That's why so many people have been run over by dominoes trucks over the years. Racing to get it there in 30 minutes. Within a number of years, he went from that little pizza joint outside of college campus. In 50 major US cities, he had 25 to 30% of the market share for all the pizza. And those 50 major metropolitan markets. On the strength of that one little proof element. That's how influential proof elements are. Especially for your marketers now out there. A copywriter has a little more limited ability to put proof elements into the package. Because a lot of these can exist from the entrepreneur or business owner. Either in the product or what the entrepreneur is willing to do. And this is very valuable for marketers and copyrights to be thinking about because you can, in effect, don't worry about the headline. Just try to make the product itself contain the proof. But there are some things you can do. Like for example, this is guaranteed. I had a direct mail package. This is from Rodale. This was a book on men's health. Cindy Burner was the marketing manager. I guess that's the right title at Rodale. But so you think that all those nice people at Rodale are just raking their organic tomatoes? Soft drinks are notoriously difficult podicaries to break into because most people are loyal to their soft drinks. Maybe I'll try. I wonder what that would taste like. A soda with mixed with a little bit of fruit juice. Maybe I'll get a better flavor out of it. So in a field notorious for tremendous failure, slice went on to capture very quickly 7% of a $30 billion a year industry on the strength of that one little proof element. Look, I can't recommend vitamins whole cloth because a lot of people taking shortcuts with the vitamins they're taking, and he becomes the person who's doing an expose. I can investigate an article on the very thing I recommend. I have to step back from that because there are too many charlottes in the marketplace. If you get my newsletter, I'll tell you the best brands to buy and so on. So becoming a champion, a consumer champion, or an expose is always a good technique to enhance your credibility. November surprise, this is one that some of our friends at Phillips tried out every presidential election cycle because it always works. And we always come up with some surprise, it's too good a headline to waste, there's got to be some surprise, Chris, as we're on, I'm talking to Chris Morett here, who's a great manager at Phillips, that every presidential cycle that seems to work, and I kind of said to, we had so many good years with this that I think he's still running it every year. He's probably in the bank for the next cycle and Chris and his team update the copy, but it always works. This was one that we did for Forecast and Strategies, I wrote for them, where it's got into all the lies that people tell you in finance. We did great caricatures of the lying stockbroker, the lying politician, lying gurus, a Darth Vader, down in the corner there is a IRS agent, and here is a typical, and it just really resonated so well with so many people who have been taken to the cleaners by, on scruples as brokers and promises that weren't kept, that was a huge hit. There's a typical sidebar for one of the, we have the politician there, you'll notice closely he's got his fingers crossed as he's taking the pledge, and I suddenly pledged to, fill in the blank, balance the federal budget, reduce the government spending, raise no new taxes, and of course he's got his fingers crossed by the stars and the flag. Another thing, this is another idea, Gary, how, please forgive me if I'm stepping on your toes, but I have learned a lot from you over the years, I have to give you credit for this one as well. Gary, as you know, has always sworn under oath that something has happened or not. Gary has a great following at every conference like this, deservedly so. This is one, I didn't want to say that our financial editor had to do anything under oath, because that might imply that he was under investigation, but we did, and this isn't that lies, lies, lies package, but we did say that Washington Insider denies that his privileged information gives investors an unfair advantage, where he says, well, I don't know, I do have a lot of information,

## [36:17 - 39:17]

you want to call it insight information? I used to work at the CIA, and I don't care if I use some of my contacts to get a little guy a break, sure. I know what the economists at the CIA and the Fed and Treasury Department, they're thinking, and I tell my readers, you want to call it insight information? Fine, but I think the little guy does need a break, and I'm going to stand for that until somebody makes me stop. So it was a great, you know, I'm for the little guy type of psychology that we captured by making him deny that it's insider information in effect. Okay, then another proof element explaining the mechanism. This was another big hit for Phillips that I wrote for Richard Bann's newsletter. I'd always been taken by the great inspirational writings of the richest man in Babylon, and I took that and kind of applied it to modern day investing that's recommended by Richard, and that was a tremendous hit. But it explained the mechanism, in other words, I just don't throw dots at a board to recommend my stocks. Here's the mechanism that I follow. I screen out all things that are overpriced. I always look for bargains and so on, and that's sold a lot of newsletters. Another example is to sell against type, another proof element rather. We saw that a little bit with the vitamin champion who now has to say, there's a great vitamin hoax going on, it just really makes people sit up and take notice. This was another way that we did it with Richard Bann, a very conservative and you would think very Republican type of person, I don't know his politics, but he sells a newsletter to people who are very conservative and well off. Here he's saying, why I'm so thrilled that Bill Clinton got elected. I know he's a Democrat and you wouldn't think of this coming from a financial advisor, but he's going to make us more money than the Republicans did, believe me, see why in sight. It's playing against type. Richard Bann has got to be a New Hampshire Republican, and here he's saying how the New Democrat is going to make us a lot of money. I mean a New Democratic President. One more example on this, John Desauer, financial advisor, was always advocating international mutual funds, international stocks, and we have him here saying the case against international mutual funds. He's well known among investors as being a champion of international investing, so when a champion of a given field says there's a case against something here, anybody interested in that subject has to be interested. Now again, with all of these ideas, please don't think that because you're not in that business, or you're not in an area of finance that these can't work for you. We're talking about psychology that works in any market, anywhere. As an example, this innocent looking case against has been for me one of the most powerful headlines ever used for anything if you make the case against something that your current market is doing.

## [39:17 - 42:25]

For example, if that had said, let's say in your mail, you got a little piece like this that says the case against attending Gary Benz of Vengus Seminar in May. I mean you'd read that because you signed up for it. Whoa, I bet what is this all about? Or even the case against traveling to New York in May. What's happening? Should I know about it? You have to do it. We had one ad in the agency I was mentioning before in our early days. I'll probably show this later so maybe I'm jumping ahead, but I want to emphasize how this is one of those formula headlines that you can use over and over again as long as you fill in the blank after the word against with something that your prospects are already doing, already interested in. It was a headline that we tested once. We all thought it would bomb, but somebody just convinced one of the copycubs convinced us to test it so we did. And it said the case against tax exempts, tax exempt bonds, this was a financial newsletter. And the other one, gold was very hot. Gold stocks were very hot at the time. And the other one said, seven gold stocks poised to soar. Now, if I ask that question, which one do you think would win? We all would have voted for the gold stocks poised to soar. The case against tax exempts beat it by multiple hundreds of a percentage. It was unbelievable. We never saw such a difference in two ads. And then when we analyzed it, we said, well, wait a minute. Tax exempt, first of all, the bond market is nine times larger than the stock market. Many more people own bonds and institutions than they own stocks. So we're talking about vast holdings of finances being addressed now. People, tax exempt bonds, a lot of widows own them and so on. Gold stocks poised to soar. Well, I'm not even into gold stocks. And it's just a tiny sliver of a much of the whole stock market, which again itself is one-ninth of the bond market. So when you have something that can be fit into that headline and that a lot of people are already into, you will get a high response, almost guaranteed if you pay it off well. And it's especially effective when it goes against type. If you have like the leading municipal bond salesman, the case against tax exempts, oh, what does he know that I don't know? He's the one who's been telling me to get into tax exempts now for 10 years. You have to read it. And you can, you know, the way to pay it off is, well, there are some that are good. There are some that are not so good. You really have to know. I can't recommend them in a blanket way anymore. So that's a way to pay that off. Okay, coming towards the end here, various proof of acknowledging disbelief. Sometimes if you have a very strong promise, the best thing you can do is acknowledge, look, I find this hard to believe myself, but here it is. So here is a mutual fund advisor. We had a big hit that I wrote for him. Believe it or not, I've discovered an investment that's safer than your bank and offers all these advantages. Now, this also is a very flexible headline combination where you have a sort of an umbrella benefit.

## [42:25 - 45:26]

You ever have, you copyrights will appreciate this, you ever have something where you can't sum up the benefit of the product or proposition in a single, you know, magical 7, 8, 12, even 25 words, this headline saves your life in that situation. You say, I've got an investment that does all this. And then you just have a laundry list of all the great advantages that it combines. And it has great, great pulling power. I've done this now with many different products across the board, consumer products, finance, health. You just have an interesting headline, that's what I call an umbrella headline. Then you name your advantages. This kind of headline is very easy to write. Announcing a blank that gives you all these advantages, combines all these advantages, and you combine the advantages under that. You can almost do that in your sleep, and it's very, very effective. But the main point I was making here is the acknowledging disbelief which buys you a lot of, oh, the writer is already bringing this to the foreground that this is hard to believe, so I don't have to bring that objection up myself. And he's being upfront with me. I know this is hard to believe, but here are the benefits. Gene Schwartz wrote a great headline in that direction, which is, let's you read that. And notice that the question mark after the question mark sort of takes a little bit of edge off, and lets you know that the writer is wondering how is this possible also. I think my favorite headline from Gene Schwartz is Rub Your Tummy Away, I've had to lose weight by rubbing it to me. Okay, another example of good proof element is highly believable source, kind of obvious. This is a great headline, pulled in a lot of sales for Silver Option dealers. And this is in fact a joint letter, this letter was sent to that man's subscriber base. So you can imagine when he is the friend of the people who on his list, it's very, very powerful when somebody who publishes a financial newsletter said, look, I didn't find this deal, but I did invest in it, and these other people are making money for me in a fantastic way. It's extremely believable because he's the believable source. And another example of that, and again this is under the column of highly believable source, is when I saw this little ad, it was kind of a poorly designed ad, it says I advise you to subscribe, I was just going through a magazine. But then I saw who it attributes to, I read each issue religiously, anyone interested in investing who doesn't subscribe is making a big mistake, and kind of buried down there is Warren Buffett. Well he's the most successful investor in the world, and what they could have done with a better graphic treatment,

## [45:26 - 48:29]

and what I have told to a number of my clients, I immediately called it maybe it was Richard Stanton Jones, Richard there's a newsletter out there that you have to acquire just to get the testimonial, because what we could do with that, oh I'm serious, it's a vastly undervalued testimonial, it's an undervalued asset about to explode with nothing more than a better graphic treatment. Warren Buffett, the greatest investor in the world, is saying I advise you to subscribe, you cannot get better than that, any copywriter who tries to write something could never have the credibility of Warren Buffett, but I don't think it was for sale and never happened, but testimonials, another type of proof element, number 10, testimonials should have this burr of singularity, they shouldn't sound like they were written by a copywriter, this was a letter that was written to one of our clients, and I was that with Dan Rosenfels, CPR and B, I'm not sure when that was written, but it was written, this is Dan Rosenfels' file of ads, I want to give him proper credit, guys like me need guys like you and our wives to protect us, now a copywriter wouldn't have thought to add that little end our wives, so it sounds like a real person wrote that, here's another example, that's a real person who wrote in how much money they made, and when they clear a million dollars I'm going to come around and buy you a lunch, and it's very believable because copywriters wouldn't have thought to say it that way, here is another one of those techniques that if you're really desperate for a good testimonial and don't have one, you can always get one from the owner of the company, but not on the outer envelope, identify him as the owner of the company, now you might think that that's kind of sneaky, but we don't have any obligation to spill all our candy in the lobby on the outside of the envelope, we can have the owner of the company graphically treated as a testimonial, he's entitled to give a testimonial about the product he passionately believes in, and in this case he's a financial publisher, and on the inside there's no, you don't really even hit a speed bump, because he starts right out saying, I'm Ron Yume, I'm so proud of this product, and it's kind of like to read it, and he thinks, oh I must have made a mistake thinking he was just an anonymous testimonial, I mean not an object of testimonial, but if you ever really need a great testimonial, dream up whatever you want, put it in the words of the owner of the company, and reveal that he's the owner on the first page of your letter, not in the outer envelope, and you've got a very powerful testimonial saying whatever you need to be said. Okay, this is my, I think my favorite category is candor, this is a category you can have all to yourself, because so few of us like to be candid and are advertising. I've been trying to teach this to Gary Hoffer for years, he just doesn't get it. You better watch out, he's sitting right behind you, Joe. Okay, this was another ad from the days when I was working with Dan Rose

## [48:29 - 51:31]

to learning this, this is a system that we learned and put together ourselves of emphasizing proof more than any other thing and certainly making it more powerful than hypey promises. This is a headline that was pulling in tremendous numbers of orders, two reasons why the price of silver may rise steeply, and the client was selling silver ingots and bars and coins rather, making a fortune because inflation was rising at that time and people were buying the silver, because the ad said, well inflation is going to put irresistible pressure on the silver prices, and as a silver shortage that's building, that's going to put further irresistible pressure on the silver prices. Very rational copy, we made a booklet available, The Case for Silver that people could send away for. It was making a fortune for this company. So I came along as a brilliant copywriter I am, I said, you know, that's kind of a wimpy headline to word, may bothers me. Why don't we just say the two reasons why the price of silver will rise steeply, let's be forthright about it, let's really put it out there. That did not perform anywhere near as well as may. Isn't that counterintuitive? Most copywriters would never write the headline that way with the word may. They would go for will or inevitable, and it seemed stronger, it's much weaker. And the tests show it, I mean that wasn't even close. This was another example when Yume hired me to sell their investment course, they wanted something really exciting, so I came back with this and they said, get rich slowly, are you serious Gary? I think you're losing a touch there because people don't want to get rich slowly. I said, believe me, I believe in this proof element marketing. Take the side of the prospect, there's so much to get rich quick hype out there. Let's sell against that, that this is the one thing you can trust because it's not going to, you know, if you're looking for a way to get rich overnight, which nobody bleeds into anyway, this is not the course for you. But if you'd like to accumulate a million dollars over the course of a normal working lifetime, that's really doable. And we'll show you how with this simple course, just putting aside maybe a thousand dollars, two thousand dollars a year in an IRA and investing it properly, you'll get there because of the power of compounding. And so here we used a number of the techniques, and this was a block, a really big blockbuster. Get rich slowly, a very believable premise, nothing to get rich quick. And we have our editor who's giving a great testimonial, it sounds like an independent, somewhat independent evaluation, the most realistic and sensible advice you'll ever read on how to get rich. And from a self-made millionaire who's also the editor of the course. And when you get inside, you'll get a sample of this also, which just goes on to say, look, hype, hype everywhere, you know it's not true, I know it's not true, let's get real. If you have in mind that you want to make a million dollars a year, you only have a couple of thousand put away each year, is there a way to do it? Let me show you the way that's worked out for me in my life and for many others through this course. And that sold a tremendous number of courses. You'll get the whole copy that you can see, all different parts of it. This is a headline test that John Capels ran.

## [51:31 - 54:31]

He too had disappointing results where they ran an ad on how to, on auto repair, how mechanics can fix cars. And the headline was fix any part of any car. And it didn't do too well. And the ad was very well written according to John Capels. So he sort of did a focus group on his own mother-in-law survey with mechanics. Why wouldn't you subscribe to this ad? Why wouldn't you buy this book? So a few mechanics looked it over and they said, well, fix any part of any car. I mean, we have some weird cars driving into our station, you know, something from Europe or Australia, something I've never seen before, a very small car that we're just not used to fixing here in the States, beautiful cars, but they don't, we're not used to handling them. So I can't believe something that's going to tell me how to fix any car, fix any part of any car. So Capels went back, changed it, fixed almost any part of almost any car. The thing was the best seller. And again, it's very counterintuitive. You would think that the second one on the bottom is weaker, but because you're thinking like marketers. If you think like a prospect who's doubting everything and looking for the quickest excuse to just blow your advertising away because it's over-promising, you're talking to that prospect in exactly the language and tenor of voice that he or she wants to hear. Finally, some of you are familiar with this ad. Again, under the category of candor, where... Back, I guess around the turn of the last century, Ernest Shackleton was a great explorer as a South Pole, and he would take these expeditions down to the Arctic. And you need a lot of funding to do that, and he would go to scientific organizations and say, well, can you sponsor this? And they'd say, well, we'll sponsor it, but you have to take a lot of scientists along so that we can advance this course of scientific knowledge. So they had to get very high-level people, doctors and scientists, to study the mammals that they would encounter, the plant life, the structure of the ice and so on. And so he had to get really high-level successful people to take this extremely hazardous journey to the South Pole from which they may never return. So you can just picture how most of us copywriters would write an ad to bring people in. We get all the benefits of that again saying, well, maybe a little dangerous, but what the heck? We'll give it a shot. But look at the power of this candor, and I tell you, if you apply this, it becomes so powerful, but we're all so afraid of it. I just love reading this ad because it reminds me every now and then of how to use this tool. Men wanted for hazardous journey, small wages, bitter cold, long months of complete darkness, constant danger, safe return doubtful, honor and recognition in case of success. Well, Shackleton said it seemed like half of England wanted to go with him. He said he could not keep up with the responses.

## [54:31 - 57:31]

There's something so powerful about admitting, was it four or five, six faults or shortcomings. No, it won't be this, it won't be that, I can't promise you that, I can't do this, but if you want to be famous, this is a chance. And so it just, wow, it just, like you said, be famous with us on this great expedition. People would think twice, what are the risks? So by laying all those risks out there, it gains great power. And the psychology behind this is the contrast principle. College teachers will do this. If they put your right hand in freezing water and your left hand in very hot water, leave them there awhile and take them out and put them in the same lukewarm water, the one that was in the hot water, the lukewarm will feel very cold, and the one that was, the same person, the one that was in the freezing, the same lukewarm water will feel very hot because of the contrast. So your two hands are feeling, this one feels very cold, this one feels very hot in the same temperature, it's a contrast principle. You can use that very effectively in advertising by admitting some faults, like you did here, that reason why that last line has such propulsion to it is because fault, fault, fault, fault, honour and recognition in case of success. And half of England seemed to respond. Five women also, they wanted to come. But he said, you know, if I have mixed genders on a six month long trip with months of darkness and everything, there's going to be a lot of rivalry, so I think we'll just go with the men. And there's also before, you know, many women had the opportunity to be in today's careers also. So anyway, just to wrap this up, here's some, finally, specialization, very obvious. Specialization is a tool you can use almost any time to make yourself more credible. If you have a brain tumor, you don't go to your family doctor, you want a really good neurosurgeon. Even better, if the tumor is on the left side of your brain, has some fancy name to it, you want the specialist who is going to really be good in that specialty. And he didn't have to sell you on letting him do the operation. I specialize in ABC, if that's what you're looking for, I'm the authority, or an authority in that field. This is what's wrong with department stores now. In an age when you try to be everything to everybody, at one time, that worked. But almost every month that goes by, you hear about another department store in trouble because they're trying to do everything for everybody in an age of specialization. If you get any time these days that you hear, try to go over to the New Time Warner Center. New York City is a perfect example of how specialization works. There's not that many departments. There's always been a need for an everything for everybody store. But most stores will succeed, or most anything will succeed, with a very narrow niche. It's much more powerful because it has so much built-in proof. If you're a brain surgeon, you don't have to sell doing the brain surgery.

## [57:31 - 1:00:34]

That's your specialization. It works for any field. When I started in business, I was so nervous that I would fail that I created 11 letterheads. I specialize in financial newsletters, I specialize in health newsletters. I specialize in financial and health newsletters. I had a whole list of things I spent. To each client, I would send the appropriate letterhead. This was before the computer was around and I couldn't do it online on my keyboard. I had to get all these polling. We had stacks of letterhead. Well, which client is this? Is this comment? I mean, it was all true. I had to specialize in each one of those things. But I wanted that client to see that I'm a specialist in exactly what you're looking for. I usually got the joke. Okay, let's look at some... In fact, I mentioned about Time Warner. I don't know what they sell there, but it's called the pink store. Everything in the store is in pink. That's how specialized some of these stores are in Manhattan. And you used to buy when you were kids, kids or PF flyers, sneakers or whatever, kids. Today, then, it's an athletic shoe store that I have tennis shoes, basketball shoes, running shoes, walking shoes, hiking shoes. I mean, it's all the specialization. And naturally, if you're a runner, you're going to want runner shoes. It sells you like that because the specialization means that's for me. That's what I want. So you wouldn't buy a basketball shoe. And so if you were a runner, even though it's probably the same shoe, it's the specialization that's making you go for one specific product. And so you can use that to your advantage. We'll see later in how to make that really help you in launching new products. Okay, we're getting close up on lunch hour, so let me just, I'm almost done. These are a couple of ads that have it all. This is one, unfortunately, I say, because it was used to sell cigarettes. This was an ad written by the great copywriter, Rossa Reeves, the first popularized, the USP. I think he may have invented it. Unique selling proposition. The four-point persuasion equation, I think, is in advance on the USP because the USP just talks about having a unique selling proposition, a promise, where it's proof because it's a tremendous clutter resulting in people tossing messages away unless there's a big, fat proof element staring right at them. Proof has now become so important, it ranks right up there with a unique promise. But this was written in the days of, and I get the instinctively knew this because it's loaded with proof, this ad, it was written in the 1950s, and you can see filtered cigarette smoke is better for your health. I guess there was scientific backing for that, I don't know. I say, unfortunately, because in all of the products, it's a shame to use all of our skills to sell bad products that are going to hurt people or hoodwink them. We should use our expertise to really help people. But this is what he wrote, and you can see just the principles involved. There's going to be an article on Rita's Digest, great proof element, one of the most trusted publications in the world. Filtred cigarette smoke is better for your health. There's a doctor down there saying something, there's another quote from an article,

## [1:00:34 - 1:03:37]

there's a mechanism that's explained. And let me just tell you what Rosserive wrote at the beginning, at the result of this campaign. With these 130 words of copy, no more, no less, monthly sales according to Wooten's annual published reports. And notice how Rosserive always writes, he always puts his proof element in. He doesn't say, according to, you know, our sales have soared, he always proves what he's saying, even a body copy, a very good habit to get into. Even in your body copy, whenever you can layer on proof, say where are these things coming from? Okay, with these 130 words of copy, no more, no less, monthly sales according to Wooten's annual published reports, jumped from 50 million to 60 million, then to 100 million, then to 150 million, all under strength of this ad, and these proof elements. 200 million and up and up and up. We are not only producing and selling now at factory capacity, we are enlarging the plant to meet consumer demands. All from manipulating proof elements. It's almost that these four elements of persuasion are kind of like puppet strings. And once you know how to use them, especially proof, you can really make the marketplace behave the way you want. Here is one that I wrote, and then we'll wrap up for this morning's session. This was an ad for, I'm going to make it a little larger so I can see it, a newsletter, Julian Snyder newsletter. So we're solving that part of the proposition. But here's where the proof really gets interesting. I think this one will rise even higher than the last one I wrote you about in January, which soars 77% in less than three months. If you don't believe me, just follow the stock in your newspaper and watch it explode. And this is not some cranky, somebody who's been called amazingly, alarmingly accurate New York Times. But if you ever get a testimony like that, you've got to put it near your claim, because here's the New York Times sort of canonizing Julian Snyder, and then you put that next to a big bowl promise. It's almost like the New York Times is promising you that this stock is going to soar. Then in the letter, it's on the back side of there, we have lots of other tips, lots of other crackerjack prizes in the mailing. And again, on the letter, we show the reader. A stock, you know, I thought it was going to go up, I think whatever percentage, it's gone even higher. But if you missed that one, on January, the year, I mailed a letter above to 238,000 investors,

## [1:03:37 - 1:04:34]

most likely yourself included in that letter. I named a stock which I said, quote, you absolutely positively must buy now. And then he gives a result to what happened. If you ignore it, I'm sorry, but I've got another one in this letter on page five that's going to do even better in my view. And that did tremendously well. It's involved in creating products and selling your services if you're a copyright designer to people, how to bring this all together for yourself. But the main point to remember is master closes secret, master closes sell on proof. The most leverage, most powerful element, because your prospects already know it's the hardest to manufacture, and therefore when they see it, it's the most prized. So of all the elements of persuasion, your prospects are looking for this just the way gold is more prized than silver. Thank you.
